MU.headline: Two-quarter beat-and-raise cadence accelerating — Q2 revenue beat guide by 28%, EPS by 45%; Q3 guide implies further +40% QoQ.

shifts.note: QoQ growth re-accelerated from +21% to +75% and is guided to +40% again
shifts.trend: Revenue $13.6B → $23.9B actual → $33.5B guide (YoY: +57% → +196% → implied +250%+)

shifts.note: Pricing power compounding — AI demand outpaces industry supply
shifts.trend: Non-GAAP GM 56.8% → 74.9% → ~81% guide (≈ +24 pts of expansion in two quarters)

shifts.note: Beat magnitude widening, not narrowing — credibility on the raised Q3 guide is high
shifts.trend: Q2 revenue guided $18.7B → printed $23.9B (+28% beat); Q3 now guided to $33.5B (+40% QoQ)

shifts.note: Same constraint language for two consecutive quarters; tightness is persistent, not transient
shifts.trend: 'Supply allocation decisions may impact certain customers' (Q1) → 'Industry supply allocation decisions continue' (Q2) — no easing language added

shifts.note: HBM hyperscaler reliance increasing — single-customer dependency to monitor
shifts.trend: One customer reached 13% of six-month revenue (Q2 disclosure) — primarily CMBU (HBM/hyperscale)

shifts.note: Singapore HBM advanced-packaging fab broke ground; capacity additions from CY2027
shifts.trend: FY26 capex ~$20B (Q1 guide); Q1 capex $5.39B + Q2 capex $5.0B = $10.4B run-rate H1 — on pace for $20B+

shifts.note: First meaningful dividend hike in years; mgmt signaling cycle durability
shifts.trend: Dividend $0.115/share (Q1) → $0.15/share (Q2, +30%)

MU.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening — beat-and-raise cadence is widening, gross-margin expansion is compounding, and guidance credibility is high. The only emerging risk is HBM customer concentration (one customer at 13% of revenue). Supply tightness language has not eased — bullish for memory pricing through CY2026.

NVDA.headline: DC growth holding ~+66–68% YoY; customer concentration formally disclosed at 4-way >10%; China is now effectively zero, but supply/energy commentary unchanged.

shifts.note: Slight re-acceleration on a full-year basis after Blackwell ramp
shifts.trend: DC YoY +66% (Q3 FY26) → +68% (FY26 full year); Networking +162% on GB200/GB300 NVLink ramp

shifts.note: Hyperscaler dependence newly visible — diligence risk-additive
shifts.trend: Q3 disclosed 4 direct customers each >10% of revenue (22/15/13/11%); 10-K did not previously break this out

shifts.note: China optionality fully impaired across both filings
shifts.trend: Q3 China $3.0B vs $8.1B prior-year Q3; H20 effectively zero by FY26 after $4.5B Q1 inventory charge; Feb 2026 H200 license carries 25% US import tariff
Non-GAAP GM 73.4% (Q3) → 73.5% guide (Q1 FY27); compressed YoY on Blackwell full-system mix but holding within target

shifts.note: Quote nearly identical across the two filings
shifts.trend: Both filings cite data-center / energy / capital availability as the gating constraint on customer deployments — no easing language added

shifts.note: Vendor-financing footprint growing — watch FCF translation
shifts.trend: FY26 10-K disclosed $17.5B in private/infra investments plus $3.5B land/power/shell guarantees to early-stage AI customers; Q3 added $7.5B new DC lease obligations FY26 Q4–FY30

NVDA.verdict: Story still about supply-constrained AI capex. The new disclosures (4-way customer concentration, vendor-financing footprint) are risk-additive but don't break the thesis; China optionality is now fully impaired and should be modeled at zero.

AMAT.headline: HBM/DRAM mix rising to offset trailing-edge logic declines, but one-time legal settlement and China erosion compress near-term margins.

shifts.trend: DRAM share of Semiconductor Systems mix: 26% (FY25) → 34% (Q1 FY26) as HBM-transition capex replaces trailing-edge logic
shifts.note: Foundry/logic fell from 67% to 62% of mix in same period — structural rotation under way

shifts.trend: China revenue $8.5B (-16% YoY, 30% of total) in FY25 → $2.1B in Q1 FY26 (-7% YoY, 30%) — absolute dollar decline persists
shifts.note: Taiwan offset: FY25 Taiwan +71% to $6.9B; Q1 FY26 Taiwan +46% — geographic rebalancing running, but China overhang not fully digested

shifts.trend: Gross margin 48.7% (FY25) → 49.0% (Q1 FY26, +0.2pts) — core margin flat; operating margin fell 4.3pts to 26.1% solely on $253M one-time BIS legal settlement
shifts.note: Absent the settlement charge, operating margin trajectory is stable; legal overhang is now cleared

shifts.trend: FY25: two customers at 19% and 15% of revenue. Q1 FY26: Taiwan +46% implies Taiwan-based leading-edge customers widening share
shifts.note: Samsung/TSMC/SK Hynix concentration risk remains elevated as HBM capex accelerates

AMAT.verdict: Thesis intact — HBM mix rotation is real and DRAM share is rising within the WFE wallet. China erosion is structural but partially offset by Taiwan leading-edge ramp. The BIS settlement was a one-time hit; the more durable concern is NAND weakness and trailing-edge logic softness, which make AMAT more cyclically exposed than peers in any memory correction.

AMD.headline: Data Center acceleration from +32% (FY25) to +57% (Q1 FY26); gross margin expanding; OpenAI 6 GW deal anchors LT demand — China still the key overhang.

shifts.trend: Data Center revenue: FY25 $16.6B (+32% YoY) → Q1 FY26 $5.8B run-rate (+57% YoY); mix rising from 48% to 56% of total revenue
shifts.note: GPU+EPYC flywheel compounding — Instinct MI350/MI450 winning hyperscaler slots alongside NVIDIA

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 50% (FY25) → 53% (Q1 FY26, +3pts YoY); Data Center op margin 22% (FY25) → 28% (Q1 FY26) on richer GPU mix
shifts.note: FY25 margin was dragged by $440M net MI308 China export charges; Q1 FY26 is the cleaner run-rate

shifts.trend: FY25: $800M MI308 charge (reversed $360M); Feb 2026: partial MI325 licenses granted; China was 22% of FY25 revenue ($7.8B)
shifts.note: MI325 licenses carry 25% US re-import tariff — China optionality partially restored at reduced economics

shifts.trend: FY25: no customer >10% of revenue; OpenAI 6 GW multi-year commitment (Oct 2025) creates de-facto concentration; Meta 20% in CRWV (an AMD cloud customer) reinforces pipeline
shifts.note: International sales jumped to 74% of Q1 FY26 revenue (vs 66% FY25) — hyperscaler shipping locations driving

shifts.trend: R&D +39% to $2.4B in Q1 FY26; ZT Systems acquisition ($3.2B) then sold manufacturing to Sanmina ($2.4B) — AMD retained design IP for Helios rack
shifts.note: Capital-light rack-design model: AMD builds the blueprint, Sanmina manufactures — structurally improves ROIC vs integrated model

AMD.verdict: Thesis strengthening. Data Center re-acceleration to +57% YoY, margin expansion to 53%, and the OpenAI 6 GW commitment provide durable demand visibility. China optionality partially restored. The main risk is whether AMD can sustain the Instinct GPU ramp cadence against NVIDIA's Blackwell/Rubin cycles — the answer so far is yes, but at materially lower operating margins than NVIDIA.

AMKR.headline: Capex tripling to $2.5-3.0B for Arizona advanced-packaging fab; Computing end-market +19% on AI/HPC; Apple concentration still 30%.

shifts.trend: Capex: $904.6M (FY25, 13.5% of sales) → $224.6M Q1 2026 alone (vs $79.9M Q1 25) → $2.5-3.0B full-year 2026 guide — 3x step-up
shifts.note: Arizona Facility (1.8M sq ft, manufacturing H1 2028) + $407M CHIPS Act direct funding drives capex surge

shifts.trend: Q1 2026 net sales $1.68B (+27.5% YoY) vs FY25 +6.2%; all four end markets growing — Computing +19%, Communications +42%, Automotive +28%
shifts.note: FY25 was depressed by inventory digestion; Q1 2026 re-acceleration confirms advanced-packaging demand is secular not cyclical

shifts.trend: Gross margin 14.2% (Q1 2026) vs 14.0% (FY25 avg) — recovering from Vietnam ramp-related compression; +2.3pts YoY
shifts.note: Margin progression will be tested by Arizona ramp costs from 2027 onward — same pattern as Vietnam compressed margins in FY25

shifts.trend: Apple 29.8% of FY25 sales (unchanged disclosure); Qualcomm 11.1%; top 10 = 72% of sales. Q1 2026: top 10 = 72% — no change
shifts.note: Apple dependence is structural — AMKR is the outsourced advanced-packaging partner for TSMC-produced Apple chips

AMKR.verdict: Thesis intact — the $2.5-3.0B capex surge is the right capital allocation given CHIPS Act funding and AI packaging demand, but execution risk is high (Arizona manufacturing only begins H1 2028). Q1 2026 +27.5% revenue rebound confirms post-inventory-correction demand. The 30% Apple concentration is the key single-customer risk; FCBGA/CoWoS/HBM diversification into AI customers is the counter-thesis driver.

AMZN.headline: AWS capex surging from $128B (2025) to $43B Q1 alone; RPO leaps to $364B after $100B OpenAI expansion; Cloud margin remains robust at 37.7%.

shifts.trend: Capex: $128.3B (FY25) → $43.2B (Q1 2026 alone) — Q1 annualizes to $170B+; FY25 capex already +65% vs FY24's $77.7B
shifts.note: Q1 2026 run-rate implies at least 35% further capex growth vs FY25; majority goes to AWS technical infrastructure

shifts.trend: AWS revenue: $128.7B FY25 (+20% YoY) → $37.6B Q1 2026 run-rate (+28% YoY) — re-acceleration of 8pts
shifts.note: AWS op margin 37.7% in Q1 vs 35.4% in FY25 — margins holding despite AI infra ramp, suggesting demand is absorbing capacity

shifts.trend: AWS RPO: $244B (Dec 31 2025) → $364B (Mar 31 2026) in one quarter, after $100B OpenAI expansion; OpenAI + Anthropic deals imply meaningful single-counterparty exposure
shifts.note: Amazon invested $15B in OpenAI + committed $35B more; $8B into Anthropic; $30B Anthropic financing facility — hyperscaler-as-VC model

shifts.trend: Revenue $189.4B (+13%) in FY25; operating income $68.6B (+86%); FCF positive at scale even amid capex surge — balance sheet absorbing build
shifts.note: AMZN unique among hyperscalers in that e-commerce/advertising cash flows fund the cloud build without dilutive equity issuance

AMZN.verdict: Thesis strongly intact. AWS re-acceleration to +28% with margins expanding to 37.7% while capex surges is the ideal combination — demand is absorbing capacity as it comes online. The OpenAI $100B expansion deal alone validates years of supply. Key watch: Trainium chip competitiveness vs NVIDIA GPUs in OpenAI workloads.

ANET.headline: AI Ethernet acceptance-period deferred revenue swelled from $2.79B to $6.20B in 5 months; revenue growing +35% but gross margin drifting down on large-customer mix.

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 $9.0B (+28.6%) → Q1 FY26 $2.71B (+35.1% YoY) — acceleration of ~7pts; binding backlog $968M at Q1 end
shifts.note: Cloud and AI Titan revenue 48% of FY25 mix; Q1 Americas rose to 84.5% vs 79.7% — hyperscaler geographic concentration

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 64.1% (FY25) → 61.9% (Q1 FY26) — 220bps compression in one quarter on large-customer pricing and silicon supply pressure
shifts.note: ANET explicitly cited memory/silicon supply tightening and large-customer pricing discounts as structural margin headwinds — not a one-quarter blip

shifts.trend: Both filings cite silicon/memory supply as a gross-margin and shipment-cadence constraint; ANET procuring ahead and managing allocation
shifts.note: Deferred revenue jump ($2.79B → $6.20B) reflects acceptance-period dynamics, not supply failure — customers are taking product but revenue recognition lags

shifts.trend: FY25: two customers at 26% and 16% of revenue; Q1 FY26: Americas 84.5% of revenue — implicitly same two hyperscalers dominating
shifts.note: AI Ethernet trials converting to production orders; once converted, deferred revenue will release into recognized revenue at scale

ANET.verdict: Thesis intact but gross margin is now the key variable to watch. The $6.2B deferred revenue pile-up is a leading indicator of future revenue, not a risk sign. The 220bps GM contraction in one quarter signals large-customer pricing power is real — long-term ANET gross margins may settle 200-300bps below FY25 levels as AI Titan volumes dominate mix.

AVGO.headline: Custom AI accelerator concentration deepening — one distributor hit 42% of Q1 revenue; semiconductor mix shift to 65% of revenue but gross margin holding at 68%.

shifts.trend: Top-5 end customers: ~40% of FY25 revenue → ~50% of Q1 FY26 revenue; one distributor: 32% of FY25 → 42% of Q1 FY26
shifts.note: The single distributor acting as channel for one hyperscaler XPU customer is the most extreme concentration disclosure in AVGO's history

shifts.trend: Semiconductor solutions: $36.9B FY25 (+22%) → $12.5B Q1 FY26 (+52% YoY); semiconductor mix: 55% FY25 → 65% Q1 FY26 of total revenue
shifts.note: Software revenue ($27B, +26%) growing but being diluted in mix by AI semi ramp; infrastructure software maintaining double-digit growth

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 68% (FY25) → 68% (Q1 FY26) — flat despite higher semi mix; semi gross margin benefit offset by AI rack/system leasing model
shifts.note: FY25 GM benefited from VMware software absorption; Q1 FY26 held at 68% is structurally impressive amid semi/rack revenue shift

shifts.trend: Operating income $25.5B FY25 (40% margin) → $8.6B Q1 FY26 (44% margin) — operating leverage improving as VMware integration matures
shifts.note: R&D +32% on stock-comp from Two-Year Equity Awards (a one-time distortion); underlying margin trajectory is expanding

AVGO.verdict: Thesis intact — AVGO's custom AI XPU business is the clearest alternative to NVDA for hyperscalers. The 42% distributor concentration in Q1 is the most acute single-customer risk in the portfolio — a sudden hyperscaler capex pause would hit AVGO harder than most. The 68% gross margin sustainability is the positive surprise; it suggests XPU economics are better than the AI rack/leasing commentary implied.

CAT.headline: Power Generation growing +32% (FY25) to +19% (Q1 FY26) as backlog jumped from $30B to $51.2B; tariffs and $1B IEEPA costs introduce margin headwinds.

shifts.trend: P&E Power Generation: FY25 +32% to $10.3B → Q1 FY26 sub-segment implied +28%; P&E segment: FY25 +12% → Q1 FY26 +22% YoY
shifts.note: Backlog: $30.0B (YE24) → $51.2B (YE25) → Q1 growth continuing; $19.3B not expected to ship in 2026 — multi-year visibility

shifts.trend: P&E margin 19.9% (FY25) → 17.7% adjusted Q1 FY26 — 220bps compression from tariff costs ($700M Q2 alone) and manufacturing inflation
shifts.note: Volume leverage +210bps offset commodity/tariff inflation -480bps; IEEPA tariff refund ($1B incurred) not deemed probable of recovery

shifts.trend: Both filings cite prime power demand from data center customers trending higher; backlog growth is structural not cyclical
shifts.note: 6-8 quarter data center backlog visibility (CMI language echoes CAT's) — demand is real and committed

shifts.trend: Feb 2026 SCOTUS ruled IEEPA tariffs unauthorized; $1.0B already incurred; recovery not probable — permanent cost increase for Q1
shifts.note: Tariff overhang: $2.2-2.4B expected for full-year FY26; Q2 alone ~$700M in Power & Energy

CAT.verdict: Thesis intact — power generation backlog doubling to $51.2B and data center prime power demand trending higher give CAT multi-year revenue visibility. Tariff headwinds are a margin drag but CAT can price through over time given lead times. Main risk is whether tariff exposure causes hyperscalers to delay power commitments in the short term.

CDNS.headline: Backlog growing $7.8B → $8.0B; China +19% to +36% YoY — BIS rescission lifted the overhang; revenue growth accelerating from +14% to +19%.

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 $5.30B (+14%) → Q1 FY26 $1.47B (+19% YoY) — 5pt acceleration; backlog $7.8B → $8.0B with 55% recognizing in next 12mo
shifts.note: Geographic diversification working: Other Americas +87%, Japan +36%, EMEA +23% in Q1 FY26 — not a US-only story

shifts.trend: China revenue: FY25 $680M (+19% YoY) → Q1 FY26 $189.4M (+36% YoY) — re-acceleration after BIS rescinded EDA license requirement Jul 2 2025
shifts.note: BIS whiplash (imposed May 23, rescinded Jul 2) did not permanently impair China; Q1 FY26 +36% shows pent-up demand released

shifts.trend: FY25 included $128.5M BIS/DOJ settlement for 2015-2021 violations (paid $140.6M); Q1 FY26 no further settlement headwind
shifts.note: Sep 2025 BIS 50%-owned-affiliate rule still creates uncertainty; company flags 'limited impact' but risk is not zero

shifts.trend: Operating margin: 28% (FY25, pressured by $128.5M settlement) → 29% (Q1 FY26) — back to FY24 levels
shifts.note: Recurring revenue mix: 82% (Q1 FY25) → 77% (Q1 FY26) as hardware and upfront licenses grew — slightly more cyclical mix

CDNS.verdict: Thesis intact. CDNS is the most direct EDA beneficiary of AI chip design complexity — every HBM, CoWoS, advanced-node SoC requires more EDA seat-months. The China re-acceleration to +36% eliminates the most bearish narrative from 2025. Watch the BIS 50%-affiliate rule risk — if triggered, China (~13% of revenue) goes to zero.

CEG.headline: Calpine $22B acquisition closed Jan 2026, scaling to 55 GW and world's largest private power producer; Crane TMI restart secured $1B DOE loan guarantee.

shifts.trend: Operating revenues: $23.6B (FY24) → $25.5B (FY25) +8% organic; Calpine adds ~$16B of gas/geo revenue base from 2026 — step-change in scale
shifts.note: Adjusted operating EPS $3.04 Q3 vs $2.74 prior year; FY25 $9.39 vs $8.67 FY24 — underlying earnings growing despite nuclear PTC headwind

shifts.trend: Q3: Calpine acquisition pending; FY25 10-K: closed at $22B (50M shares + $4.5B cash Jan 7 2026); Crane $1B DOE loan guarantee Nov 2025 adds TMI restart financing
shifts.note: 55 GW total fleet post-Calpine vs ~32 GW pre-acquisition; more than doubles addressable hyperscaler PPA base

shifts.trend: NY PSC approved 20-year ZEC extension for NY nuclear fleet Jan 2026; Crane DOE loan at +37.5bps to UST (favorable rate) locked in
shifts.note: Meta 20-year PPA for Clinton Clean Energy signed June 2025 — first FoA hyperscaler nuclear PPA for CEG

shifts.trend: Calpine acquisition funded with $4.5B cash + 50M CEG shares — meaningful leverage addition at consolidated level; Calpine's own debt portfolio comes along
shifts.note: Net nuclear PTC revenue declined YoY (IRA phasedown schedule) — partially offsetting operating earnings growth

CEG.verdict: Thesis structurally strengthened — Calpine acquisition makes CEG the de facto owner of US baseload power assets that hyperscalers need under long-term PPAs. Crane TMI restart with DOE financing de-risks the nuclear restart thesis. The main financial risk is the Calpine leverage import and nuclear PTC revenue decline. For AI investing, CEG is now effectively the infrastructure layer between renewable/nuclear power and hyperscaler data centers.

CLS.headline: HPS revenue +81% to $5.0B (41% of FY25 total) on networking switch ramps; Q1 FY26 +53% YoY with Enterprise +101% on next-gen AI/ML compute restart.

shifts.note: Enterprise end-market +101% in Q1 FY26 on hyperscaler AI/ML compute restart (same customer that paused in 2025) — confirms inventory digestion complete

shifts.trend: FY25: 3 CCS customers each over 10% (32%, 14%, 12%); top 10 = 79% of revenue; Q1 FY26 revolver upsized to $1.75B to fund scale-up
shifts.note: 32% customer is the dominant risk — any pause ripples through the entire CLS P&L given CCS is 80% of revenue

shifts.trend: CCS segment margin: 8.4% Q4 FY25 → 8.6% Q1 FY26 (+20bps) on operating leverage; ATS segment 6.0%
shifts.note: Gross margins in EMS are structurally thin; expansion is real but incremental; Q1 FY26 adjusted EPS $2.16 beat high end of guide

shifts.trend: FY26 capex guided ~$1B (~6% of revenue) vs historical 1.5-2.0%; revolver upsized $750M to $1.75B; Term A refinanced to $250M/April 2031
shifts.note: Capex acceleration is a prerequisite for capturing hyperscaler capacity — if demand pauses, capex commitment becomes a liability

CLS.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening. CLS is the cleanest EMS proxy on AI hyperscaler networking capex. Q1 FY26 +53% with Enterprise +101% (the restart of the previously-paused AI/ML compute program) confirms the FY25 trough was temporary. Key risk: 32% customer concentration — CLS is effectively a derivative of that single hyperscaler's spend decisions.

CMI.headline: Power Systems EBITDA margin expanded from 22.7% (FY25) to 29.5% (Q1 FY26) on data center power generation; 6-8 quarter DC backlog visibility maintained.

shifts.trend: Power Systems: FY25 $7.46B (+16%) → Q1 FY26 $1.96B run-rate (+19%); Power Generation sub-line: FY25 $4.73B (+19%) → Q1 FY26 $1.28B (+28%)
shifts.note: International +16% in Q1 FY26 on China + Europe power generation strength; US/Canada -6% on weak truck demand — DC demand is global

shifts.trend: Power Systems EBITDA: FY25 $1.69B (22.7% margin) → Q1 FY26 $577M (29.5% margin) — 680bps expansion in one quarter
shifts.note: Q1 margin lift from volumes, operational leverage, lower net tariff costs, and pricing; Distribution segment EBITDA also up from DC power generation

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: 'strong demand for data center products extending 6-8 quarters out'; Q1 FY26 10-Q: same language maintained — no backlog deceleration
shifts.note: 6-8 quarter backlog in both reports = credible multi-year visibility; capacity ramp ongoing

shifts.trend: Engine segment: Q1 FY26 EBITDA -$179M YoY on lower truck volumes and higher product coverage costs — drag offsetting Power Systems strength
shifts.note: Truck demand weak (H1 expected); $458M Accelera charges booked FY25 from hydrogen exit — diversification away from clean energy bet

CMI.verdict: Thesis intact. Power Systems is compounding at high-teen/low-20% growth with margins now at 29.5% EBITDA — exceptional quality. The 6-8 quarter DC backlog visibility provides durable revenue. Engine segment weakness is a headwind but not a thesis-breaker. Main structural risk: if hyperscalers shift to utility-scale connections (vs backup power), CMI's data center opportunity shrinks.

COHR.headline: D&C segment accelerating from +34% to +41% YoY as gross margin expands to 38%; first explicit 'scale across' inter-DC interconnect mention adds new demand vector.

shifts.trend: D&C segment: Q2 FY26 +34% → Q3 FY26 +41% YoY; D&C profit: Q2 $306M → Q3 $348M (+49% Q3 YoY); 9M D&C profit +39% to $923M
shifts.note: New Q3 comment: 'scale across' inter-DC interconnect joining DCI and telecom as demand drivers — addressable market expanding

shifts.trend: Gross margin: Q2 37% (+140bps) → Q3 38% (+243bps YoY) — sequential improvement as D&C yield and pricing optimization continue
shifts.note: Industrial divestitures (Munich Jan 26, aerospace Sep 25) improving consolidated margins by removing lower-margin mix

shifts.trend: Industrial segment: Q2 -10% → Q3 -16% — continuing to shrink post-divestiture, but COHR explicitly narrowing to AI/DC optics as the core
shifts.note: Portfolio simplification toward D&C is structurally margin-positive and valuation-multiple-positive

shifts.trend: Lower interest expense (-$13M Q3, -$39M YTD) on term-loan paydown; EPS diluted $1.95 vs $0.41 prior year (+375%) on combined operating + financial leverage
shifts.note: Tax rate 4% YTD vs 14% prior — German tax law changes and stock-comp windfalls further boosted reported EPS

COHR.verdict: Thesis strengthening. D&C acceleration to +41% with gross margin at 38% and rising represents a genuine structural re-rating. The 'scale across' language is new and material — if inter-DC AI fabric becomes as large as intra-DC AI, COHR's addressable market doubles. Portfolio simplification is the right strategic move. Execution on yield and pricing is tracking ahead of plan.

CRM.headline: FY26 RPO grew $72.4B (+14%) with cRPO outpacing (+16%); $9.6B Informatica acquisition closes; Agentforce platform revenue the only double-digit growth segment.

shifts.trend: Revenue: Q3 FY26 $10.26B (+9%) → FY26 $41.5B (+10%); RPO: $59.5B (Oct 31) → $72.4B (Jan 31) — sequential RPO rebuild suggests deceleration reversed
shifts.note: cRPO +16% outpacing total RPO +14% — accelerating front-book vs back-book; FY27 setup improving

shifts.trend: Agentforce 360 Platform/Slack/Other: Q3 +19% to $2.18B vs Agentforce Sales +8%, Service +9% — the AI consumption bucket is the fastest-growing segment
shifts.note: Informatica $0.4B revenue contributed in FY26; Data 360 + Agentforce creates the unified AI/data layer that competes with Snowflake + ServiceNow

shifts.trend: Q3 RPO $59.5B (temporarily depressed from Jan 31 $63.4B); FY26 $72.4B (+14%); operating cash flow $15.0B (+15%); $12.7B stock repurchase + $1.6B dividend
shifts.note: CRM returning $14.3B to shareholders in FY26 while absorbing $9.6B Informatica — suggests high confidence in FCF generation

shifts.trend: Operating margin: Q3 20% → FY26 full year 20% (vs 19% FY25) — steady improvement; diluted EPS $7.80 vs $6.36
shifts.note: Agentforce consumption pricing creates upside optionality if AI agents prove productivity-additive to customers

CRM.verdict: Thesis intact. CRM's Agentforce platform is the early leader in enterprise AI agent orchestration. The Informatica deal is strategically coherent (data layer + AI agents). cRPO +16% is the most bullish leading indicator. Main risk: AI agent revenue model (consumption-based) is uncertain in scale — Agentforce remains relatively small vs the core subscription base.

CRWV.headline: RPO $60.7B → $98.8B in one quarter as Meta and OpenAI ramp; Microsoft concentration fell from 67% to 45%; but $21.6B debt at 9-15% is a structural solvency risk.

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 $5.1B (+168%) → Q1 2026 $2.08B run-rate (+112% YoY); RPO: $60.7B (Dec 31) → $98.8B (Mar 31) in one quarter
shifts.note: Microsoft fell from 67% to 45% as Meta emerged as 20% customer — diversification running ahead of plan

shifts.trend: Microsoft: 77% (FY24 top-2) → 67% (FY25) → 45% (Q1 2026); Meta: 0% → 20% in Q1 2026 — two major customer diversification in 6 months
shifts.note: OpenAI $11.9B committed through Oct 2030 + $6.5B Sept 2025 order; Meta $14.2B through Dec 2031 — concentration still extreme even post-diversification

shifts.trend: Debt: FY25 $21.6B at 9-15% avg → Q1 2026 added $4.0B converts + $2.8B senior notes + $1.0B equity — total debt approaching $30B
shifts.note: Interest expense will consume a significant portion of revenue growth; at $33.5B RPO in next 24 months vs $30B+ debt, solvency is workable but tight

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $10.3B; 850 MW active → 3.1 GW contracted; Q1 2026: $1.2B in new data center JV equity stakes; GB200/GB300 NVL72 first-to-market
shifts.note: CRWV is effectively building GPU-dense data centers on leveraged balance sheet — P/L is a function of power contract timing and GPU utilization

CRWV.verdict: High-conviction, high-risk thesis. CRWV's RPO growth from $15B to $99B in 15 months is extraordinary and validates the hyperscaler-grade AI infrastructure demand. The debt at 9-15% is the structural constraint — CRWV must convert RPO to cash revenue before debt service consumes FCF. The Microsoft deconcentration is working. The JV model for data center equity is clever but adds off-balance-sheet complexity.

CSCO.headline: Networking +15% to +21% across two quarters on AI Infrastructure; Silicon One capacity commitments building; Security still soft on Splunk cloud transition.

shifts.trend: Networking product category: Q1 FY26 +15% (+$1.0B) → Q2 FY26 +21% (+$1.4B); 1H FY26 total revenue $30.23B +9%
shifts.note: Service Provider and Cloud customer market driving growth across Americas and EMEA — AI infrastructure orders are the engine

shifts.trend: Q1: inventory +7% and purchase commitments +10% tied to Silicon One capacity for hyperscalers; Q2: inventory buildup continuing as Silicon One contract manufacturing ramps
shifts.note: CSCO flagged Silicon One commitments raise supply-chain obsolescence risk — inventory build is a double-edged sword

shifts.trend: Security category: Q1 -4% on Splunk shift to cloud subscriptions; Q2 -4% continuing — ongoing headwind expected through 2H FY26
shifts.note: Splunk cloud migration converting perpetual license revenue to recurring subscription — temporarily depresses reported revenue

shifts.trend: Product gross margin: Q1 -0.6pts; Q2 +0.2pts — net flat as productivity offsetting mix pressure; diluted EPS Q2 +31% on tax rate benefit
shifts.note: Tax rate Q2 12.9% vs 15.9% prior year contributed to EPS acceleration; normalized EPS growth slower

CSCO.verdict: Thesis intact. Cisco is the legacy networking incumbent capturing AI Infrastructure demand through Silicon One. The Networking re-acceleration to +21% in Q2 vs +15% in Q1 is a positive second derivative. Security weakness is a known headwind from Splunk integration and is temporary. Main risk: if open-standard AI Ethernet (ANET) displaces Silicon One in hyperscaler switch buys, CSCO's AI upside shrinks.

ETN.headline: Electrical Americas backlog +31% to +44% YoY organic; orders +42% organic Q1; $9.5B Boyd Thermal acquisition adds liquid cooling to the electrification portfolio.

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas backlog: $13.2B (YE25, +31% organic) → $14.5B (Q1 end, +44% organic); book-to-bill 1.2x maintained
shifts.note: Backlog growth accelerating quarter-over-quarter — demand coming in faster than ETN can build capacity

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas: FY25 $13.3B (+16%) → Q1 FY26 $3.60B run-rate (+20%); Electrical Global: FY25 $6.8B (+9%) → Q1 FY26 $1.95B (+21%)
shifts.note: Electrical Global added to data center commentary in Q1 — global AI buildout reaching international markets

shifts.trend: Electrical Americas op margin: 29.9% (FY25) → 25.6% (Q1 FY26) — 480bps compression from commodity/tariff inflation exceeding volume leverage
shifts.note: 480bps commodity hit offset by only 210bps sales leverage — pricing power insufficient to fully absorb input cost inflation in the near term

shifts.trend: FY25 acquisitions: Fibrebond (DC power enclosures), Resilient (solid-state transformers); Nov 2025: $9.5B Boyd Thermal agreement — liquid cooling entry
shifts.note: Boyd Thermal fills the liquid cooling gap; ETN is building a chip-to-grid data center electrical portfolio

ETN.verdict: Thesis intact — ETN's data center electrical infrastructure backlog is structural and accelerating. The Boyd Thermal acquisition is strategically important (liquid cooling is the next high-growth layer). The margin compression in Q1 is the key watch item — commodity/tariff inflation squeezing 480bps is significant, and pricing power needs to catch up.

FN.headline: Revenue accelerating from +35.9% to +39.3% YoY; DCI (inter-DC AI fabric) +90.4% is the fastest-growing optical line; HPC now $106.7M a quarter.

shifts.note: DCI +90.4% in Q3 vs Q2 — inter-datacenter AI interconnect is the highest-growth optical category at Fabrinet

shifts.trend: DCI: Q2 $142.2M → Q3 $196.9M (+38.5% QoQ); HPC: Q2 $85.6M → Q3 $106.7M (+24.6% QoQ); both secular AI demand vectors
shifts.note: Telecom $431.4M (+42.5% YoY) now largest optical line — traditional carrier buildout still growing alongside AI interconnect

shifts.trend: Gross margin: Q2 12.2% → Q3 11.9% (-30bps) on customer mix; operating margin: Q2 10.1% → Q3 9.9% — stable within thin EMS band
shifts.note: Operating income +52.1% in Q3 on revenue scale — absolute dollar growth despite flat % margin

shifts.trend: Q2: 4 customers each >10% (combined 59.1%) vs 2 customers in prior year; Q3: same 4-customer disclosure maintained
shifts.note: Nokia/Infinera + Lumentum/NeoPhotonics/Cisco/Acacia consolidation waves created the concentrated FN customer base

FN.verdict: Thesis intact. FN is the highest-quality pure-play on optical interconnect manufacturing — the outsourced production house for the entire optical transceivers and components industry. DCI +90.4% confirms AI scale-across demand is real and large. Margin stability in an EMS context is a positive; the operating leverage at revenue scale is where the earnings story sits.

GEV.headline: Heavy-Duty Gas Turbine orders 110 units in 2025 (vs 68 in 2024); total RPO hit $163.3B in Q1 (+32% YoY); Gas Power EBITDA margin expanded to 16.3%.

shifts.trend: Total RPO: $150.2B (YE25) → $163.3B (Q1 end) — $13B sequential build; Power RPO $94.4B → $99.7B; equipment RPO $64.2B → $75.9B vs $45.5B YoY
shifts.note: HA-Turbine orders: 43 units FY25 vs 25 FY24 vs 8 FY23 — exponential acceleration; the supply bottleneck is GEV's manufacturing capacity

shifts.trend: FY25 revenue $38.1B (+9%); Q1 2026 revenue $9.3B (+16% YoY); Gas Power EBITDA: FY25 14.7% → Q1 16.3%
shifts.note: Q1 FCF $4.8B vs $1.0B — massive FCF expansion on favorable pricing + higher volume + milestone deliveries

shifts.trend: Prolec GE acquired Feb 2026 ($5.3B, 10,000-employee transformer JV); Electrification RPO gaining from Prolec + HVDC/synchronous condenser demand
shifts.note: First commercial SMR contract in North America (Hitachi JV) — nuclear optionality being established alongside gas power scale

shifts.trend: HA-Turbine GW orders: FY24 20.2 GW → FY25 29.8 GW (+47%); Q1 2026 HA-Turbine orders 12 units vs 8 prior year — 2026 pacing above FY25
shifts.note: Fitch upgraded to BBB+, S&P to BBB (positive outlook) on improved financial profile — cost of capital improving

GEV.verdict: Thesis strongly intact — GEV is a direct beneficiary of data-center-driven power demand, with RPO at $163B and growing. The 10-year order pipeline for gas turbines is extraordinary. The Prolec GE transformer acquisition fills the grid-connection bottleneck. Main risk: geopolitical supply chains for gas turbine components (40+ countries); also SMR remains pre-commercial.

GNRC.headline: C&I segment +28% Q1 on data center; large-megawatt backup generator market 'significantly supply-constrained'; Enercon acquisition adds hyperscale custom power.

shifts.trend: C&I segment: FY25 +4.9% to ~$3.5B → Q1 FY26 $510M (+28% YoY); data center cited as primary growth driver in both periods
shifts.note: FY25 total sales -2% on residential softness; Q1 FY26 total +12% as C&I growth overcomes residential flat

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: large-megawatt backup generator market 'significantly supply-constrained'; Q1 FY26: C&I ~10% net content from tariffs absorbed — demand not wavering
shifts.note: Supply constraint is bullish for pricing power; GNRC's large-megawatt genset launch in 2025 directly targets this gap

shifts.trend: Adjusted EBITDA: FY25 $716M (17.0% margin) → Q1 FY26 $193M (18.2% run-rate, +29% YoY); C&I margin 13.0% (Q1 26) vs 11.4% prior year
shifts.note: C&I margin expansion from 11.4% to 13.0% confirms pricing power in constrained large-genset market

shifts.trend: Segments reorganized from Domestic/International to Residential/C&I in Q1 FY26; Enercon acquired for hyperscale custom power and industrial enclosures
shifts.note: Enercon acquisition builds out the hyperscale-specific power solutions portfolio — moving up the stack from commodity gensets

GNRC.verdict: Thesis intact. GNRC's C&I data center power opportunity is large and supply-constrained. Q1 FY26 +28% with margin expansion to 13% confirms pricing power. The large-megawatt genset launch and Enercon acquisition position GNRC for the hyperscale custom power market that was previously out of reach. Main risk: residential weakness in large outage-free years creates a volatile revenue baseline.

GOOGL.headline: Google Cloud revenue $20.0B (+63%) and op margin 33% in Q1; RPO $467.6B after multi-GW TPU hardware supply agreements booked; capex $35.7B in Q1 alone.

shifts.trend: Google Cloud: FY25 $58.7B (+36%) → Q1 2026 $20.0B run-rate (+63% YoY) — massive re-acceleration; Cloud op margin 33% vs 23.7% FY25 avg
shifts.note: Cloud op income $6.6B vs $2.2B prior year (+200%) — profitability growing 3x as fast as revenue; scale is inflecting

shifts.trend: Capex: FY25 $91.4B (+74% vs FY24) → Q1 2026 $35.7B (annualizing to $140B+); management: 'significantly increase' in 2026 vs 2025
shifts.note: First-time disclosure: 'multi-gigawatt' TPU hardware supply agreements signed in Q1; revenue starts late 2026, majority 2027

shifts.trend: Revenue backlog: $242.8B (Dec 31 2025) → $467.6B (Mar 31 2026) — nearly doubled in one quarter after multi-GW TPU deals booked
shifts.note: Wiz ($29.5B) and Intersect ($5.9B) closed in March 2026; $40B committed to a private company (likely AI startup) in March

shifts.trend: Issued $31.1B notes Q1 2026; $37.3B in 2025; $16B into Waymo Feb 2026; balance sheet absorbing multi-hundred billion investment program
shifts.note: GOOGL monetizes investments via advertising scale; Cloud margin expansion funding capex build — structurally self-financing

GOOGL.verdict: Thesis intact and accelerating. Google Cloud's +63% with 33% op margin is the most bullish cloud print in the sector. The multi-GW TPU supply deal creates a new category: hyperscalers as GPU-equivalent hardware buyers from Alphabet itself. RPO doubling in one quarter to $467.6B is extraordinary demand visibility. The main risk is antitrust — DOJ advertising case still pending.

HPE.headline: Juniper integration driving Networking +51% to +151%; AI server backlog significant but GPU-heavy mix carries 'limited margins'; AI Systems ARR still nascent.

shifts.trend: Networking: FY25 +51.1% ($2.3B from Juniper) → Q1 FY26 +151.5% (+$1.6B, full Juniper quarter); total revenue Q1 FY26 +18.4%
shifts.note: Full-quarter Juniper inclusion in Q1 FY26 creates a high comp for future quarters; organic Networking growth to be watched vs inorganic

shifts.trend: Gross margin: FY25 ~28.5% (non-GAAP) + $1.6B goodwill impairment → Q1 FY26 35.9% (+670bps) on Juniper revenue mix
shifts.note: AI servers carry 'limited margins' per mgmt — GPU-heavy AI server mix is margin-dilutive; Juniper networking mix is margin-accretive

shifts.trend: ARR: $3.15B (+63% YoY) at Q1 FY26 driven by Networking from Merger; 'significant AI backlog' exists but lumpy deals and memory shortages flagged
shifts.note: Cloud & AI segment (new combined Server + Storage) -2.7% in Q1 — legacy server headwinds offsetting AI ramp

shifts.trend: Juniper integration costs $123M in Q1; $600M synergy target by FY28 requires $800M investment; $275M Catalyst program charges in FY25
shifts.note: FY25 GAAP operating loss $437M from $1.6B goodwill impairment + acquisition costs; non-GAAP margin 12.7% (+280bps) is the cleaner metric

HPE.verdict: Thesis is mixed. Juniper was the right acquisition for AI networking exposure, but the integration cost ($800M investment for $600M synergies) and the AI server margin challenge ('limited margins' on GPU systems) are structural headwinds. The 63% ARR growth from Networking is positive. Main watch: whether Juniper's AI-native campus/enterprise networking can compete with Arista in AI infrastructure switching.

HUBB.headline: Electrical Solutions organic +7.2% (FY25) to +10.6% (Q1 FY26) on datacenter; Utility Solutions T&D steady; margins recovering.

shifts.trend: Electrical Solutions: FY25 $2.17B (+7.1% organic) → Q1 FY26 $568M (+10.6% organic); datacenter cited as primary driver in both periods
shifts.note: Utility Solutions organic also improving: FY25 +1.1% → Q1 FY26 +6.8% on T&D market strength

shifts.trend: Adjusted operating margin: FY25 22.5% (+80bps) → Q1 FY26 +110bps overall; Electrical Solutions Q1 -30bps (commodity/tariff) offset by volume
shifts.note: Overall margin improvement despite Electrical Solutions segment compression — Utility Solutions margin expansion more than compensating

shifts.trend: FY25: $958M in acquisitions to expand product reach; backlog $2.16B (+14% YoY), substantially all shipping in 2026
shifts.note: HUBB is a bolt-on acquirer; FCF $875M in FY25 provides acquisition currency without leverage strain

HUBB.verdict: Thesis intact. HUBB's datacenter electrical exposure is real and growing. Organic Electrical Solutions growth accelerating from 7.2% to 10.6% confirms demand. The Utility T&D exposure is a diversifier that provides ballast. Main risk: HUBB is a smaller-scale play on the data center electrical infrastructure theme compared to ETN/VRT; less direct hyperscaler exposure.

INTC.headline: Foundry losses persisting at $2.3-2.4B/quarter; supply constraints hitting CCG/DCAI in Q1 2026; 14A pause threat de-risked by NVIDIA $5B investment and USG escrow.

shifts.trend: DCAI: FY25 $14.3B (+794M YoY) → Q1 2026 $5.1B (+22% YoY) on premium server ASP mix; Intel Foundry external: FY25 $307M → Q1 2026 $174M external (Altera)
shifts.note: DCAI +22% in Q1 is the strongest growth rate in years — Gaudi/Xeon ramp contributing; supply constraints limited volume further upside

shifts.trend: Gross margin: FY25 ~37% → Q1 2026 39.4% (+2.5pts); Foundry operating loss: FY25 $10.3B → Q1 2026 $2.4B run-rate — still losing but at steady pace
shifts.note: Gross margin recovery on premium DCAI/CCG mix; 18A ramp costs partially offset by lower lower-of-cost reserves

shifts.trend: Both filings: Intel 7 and multi-node supply constraints limiting CCG/DCAI; Q1 2026: 'substrate, memory, other components' adding to internal constraints
shifts.note: Supply constraints throughout 2026 (especially H1) — Intel's own inventory tightening limits external sales, not just internal production

shifts.trend: NVIDIA $5B equity investment, SoftBank $2B (both 2025); USG 159M-share escrow ($3.2B CHIPS disbursements); Apollo SCIP buyout Q2 2026
shifts.note: Strategic investors restructuring Intel's capital — NVIDIA partnership to co-develop x86+NVIDIA AI products is the most bullish long-term signal

INTC.verdict: Turnaround thesis — high risk, binary optionality. The NVIDIA partnership + USG escrow + SoftBank investment provide a credible restructuring path. But Intel Foundry external revenue is still negligible ($174M/quarter) and the loss is $2.4B/quarter. The 14A pause risk is existential for Intel's long-term foundry ambitions. The Q1 2026 DCAI +22% print is the most positive data point in years.

KLAC.headline: Revenue +7% to +11% YoY on HBM/DRAM-led memory spend; Korea +34% to +80% as Samsung ramps; China declining from 30% to 24% of revenue.

shifts.trend: Total revenue: Q2 FY26 $3.30B (+7% YoY) → Q3 FY26 $3.42B (+11% YoY) — sequential re-acceleration; services +16-18% across both quarters
shifts.note: Record revenue in Q3 FY26; HBM/DRAM memory spend is the primary engine — foundry/logic steady

shifts.trend: Korea revenue: Q2 +34% YoY → Q3 +80% YoY on memory investment surge; North America +38% to +40%; Taiwan -12% in Q3 on shipment timing
shifts.note: Korea surge = SK Hynix and Samsung HBM4 qualification spend — direct read-through to HBM supply ramp

shifts.trend: China: Q2 $995M (30.2% of revenue, -9% YoY) → Q3 $830M (24.2% of revenue, +5% YoY in absolute but mix declining)
shifts.note: US export controls structurally constraining China WFE spend; modest +5% absolute in Q3 masks continued share erosion

shifts.trend: Gross margin: Q2 61.4% (+1.1pts YoY) → Q3 61.1% (-0.5pts QoQ) — DRAM chip costs in image computers transitorily pressuring; mgmt frames as temporary
shifts.note: Services 23-24% of revenue and growing — recurring revenue mix improving; 16th/17th consecutive annual dividend increases

KLAC.verdict: Thesis intact. KLAC is the process-control incumbent with 60%+ gross margins that rise with technology-node complexity. HBM/DRAM investment surge is directly driving Korea +80%. China exposure declining but manageable. The DRAM chip input cost pressure on image computers is a transitory margin headwind that mgmt is clear about. Main risk: if HBM demand plateaus in 2027, KLAC faces a memory capex digestion cycle.

LEU.headline: $900M DOE HALEU task order won Jan 2026; Piketon expansion EPC signed with Fluor; Oklo JV exploration announced; backlog growing to $3.9B.

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 (full year data not in 10-K summary) → Q1 FY26 $76.7M (+5%); SWU spot price $200 vs $34 historic low — pricing environment remains tight
shifts.note: Q1 FY26 LEU revenue -13% on lower SWU volume (-47%) offset by higher price (+52%) — supply rationing driving ASP up

shifts.trend: Non-Russian enrichment market is undersupplied; Russian TENEX = 43% of global capacity; Centrus only active HALEU producer with NRC license
shifts.note: SWU spot at $200 vs $34 historic low (+488%) — structural undersupply positioning Centrus to monetize capacity investments

shifts.trend: FY25 10-K: $900M HALEU task order + $560M+ Oak Ridge centrifuge investment; Q1 FY26: $24M Fluor EPC signed; Oklo JV exploration; advanced tech costs +530% to $18.9M
shifts.note: Piketon expansion will produce both LEU and HALEU; co-location with Oklo's 1.2 GW Pike County power campus is strategic

shifts.trend: Aug 2025: $805M 0% convertibles due 2032 issued; 8.25% notes redeemed March 2025; $62.4M IRS §48C credit for Oak Ridge
shifts.note: 0% converts are capital-efficient; backlog $3.8B → $3.9B provides revenue visibility to service the capital program

LEU.verdict: Niche thesis — Centrus is the only company with a NRC HALEU license and active production, which positions it uniquely for SMR fuel supply. The Oklo JV exploration is the most interesting development — co-located HALEU production + SMR power could be a vertically integrated nuclear utility model for data centers. Main risk: pre-commercial scale and execution dependency on DOE funding.

LITE.headline: Revenue +65.5% to +90.1% YoY; gross margin expanded 1,130 to 1,540bps in two quarters; supply allocation language hardened — LITE is the InP choke point for 1.6T.

shifts.trend: Revenue: Q2 FY26 $665.5M (+65.5%) → Q3 FY26 $808.4M (+90.1% YoY); 9M $2.01B (+72.4%) — acceleration continuing
shifts.note: Systems growth Q3 +121.1% vs Components +77.3% — cloud transceiver modules (Systems) accelerating faster than laser chips

shifts.trend: Gross margin: Q2 36.1% (+1,130bps) → Q3 44.2% (+1,540bps) — 810bps sequential improvement; 9M 38.8% (+1,300bps)
shifts.note: 200G lane-speed mix lifting ASPs; supply allocation enabling price discipline; optical circuit switches >$25M in Q3 at higher margin

shifts.trend: Q2: 'demand is outpacing current supply which has led to decisions on supply allocation'; Q3: language hardened — 'has required us to make decisions on supply allocation'
shifts.note: This language shift from 'has led to' to 'has required' indicates tighter allocation management — LITE is explicitly rationing supply

shifts.trend: Q2: two customers at 24% and 17% (combined 41%); Q3: two customers at 26% and 12% (combined 38%) — top customer share rose 2pts
shifts.note: Hyperscaler optical transceiver demand concentrating around 1-2 dominant buyers in each generation cycle

LITE.verdict: Thesis strongly intact — LITE may be the most acute supply choke point in the AI inference stack. The 200G InP/EML laser chip is the building block for 1.6T transceivers, and only a handful of suppliers globally (LITE, II-VI) can manufacture at scale. The gross margin re-rating from 36% to 44% in one quarter is structural, not cyclical — supply allocation pricing power is real.

LRCX.headline: DRAM investment surge drove Q3 +9% QoQ with memory mix rising from 34% to 39%; Korea revenue jumped to 23%; gross margin stable at ~50%.

shifts.trend: Revenue: Q2 FY26 $5.34B (flat QoQ, +25% YoY for H1) → Q3 FY26 $5.84B (+9% QoQ); nine-month $16.51B vs $13.26B (+24%)
shifts.note: DRAM customer investment surge drove the sequential re-acceleration — HBM capacity addition spending flowing through LRCX

shifts.trend: Memory mix: 34% Q2 → 39% Q3 on DRAM/NVM investment; Foundry: 59% Q2 → 54% Q3 — foundry slipping as memory accelerates
shifts.note: Korea revenue: 20% Q2 → 23% Q3; Taiwan: 23% both quarters; China 35% → 34% — memory customer geography driving the mix

shifts.trend: China: 43% Sep qtr (Q1 FY26) → 35% Dec qtr (Q2 FY26) → 34% Mar qtr (Q3 FY26) — declining from peak but stabilizing
shifts.note: US export controls constraining China WFE penetration; non-China DRAM/foundry spend offsetting

shifts.trend: Gross margin: 49.6% (Q2, -0.8pts QoQ) → 49.8% (Q3, +0.2pts QoQ); six-month 50.0% (+2.3pts YoY) — stable around 50%
shifts.note: Customer-support revenue growing (+6% QoQ) from installed-base expansion — durable services revenue improving quality

LRCX.verdict: Thesis intact. LRCX is a pure-play WFE beneficiary of DRAM/HBM investment cycles. The Q3 +9% QoQ re-acceleration on DRAM spend is a direct read-through to HBM4 capacity additions at SK Hynix and Micron. Korea +3pts mix shift confirms the narrative. China at 34% is a structural risk if export controls tighten further — LRCX has more China exposure than KLAC.

META.headline: FY26 capex guidance raised from $115-135B to $125-145B in one quarter; $237.7B in non-cancelable commitments; ad revenue +33% with impressions +19%.

shifts.trend: Capex: FY25 $72.2B → FY26 guide $115-135B at year-end → raised to $125-145B in Q1 → Q1 actual $19.8B annualizing to $79B baseline
shifts.note: Off-balance-sheet leases not yet commenced = $182.9B (data centers, colocation, network infra) starting 2026-2036 — total infra commitment enormous

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 $201.0B (+22%) → Q1 2026 $56.3B (+33% YoY) — acceleration on ad pricing and impressions
shifts.note: Q1 2026 operating income $22.9B (+30%); RL operating loss $4.03B — AI/hardware R&D spending at scale while core monetizing

shifts.trend: Meta's non-cancelable commitments: $237.7B at Mar 31 ($42.3B due 2026); April 2026 added another $24B in multi-year infra contracts
shifts.note: META is a major demand driver across the inference stack — GPU procurement, liquid cooling, networking, power — as buyer, not supplier

shifts.trend: RL operating loss: FY25 $19.2B → Q1 FY26 $4.03B run-rate ($16B annualized) — RL costs not declining; effective tax rate 30% vs 12% in FY24 from OBBBA
shifts.note: OBBBA July 2025 tax law created $15.93B charge in FY25; structural tax rate shift from 12% to 30% permanently increases cost of capital

META.verdict: Thesis intact — META's ad business is accelerating on AI-enhanced ad targeting. The $125-145B FY26 capex guidance + $182.9B uncommenced leases + $237.7B non-cancelable commitments = extraordinary demand for all layers of the AI inference stack. RL losses and higher effective tax rate are the structural headwinds. META is best analyzed as an AI infrastructure demand signal, not a supply play.

MOD.headline: Climate Solutions data center revenue +$67M (Q2) → +$130M (Q3) YoY; RMT spin/merge with Gentherm announced to create pure-play DC cooling co.

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions data center revenue: Q2 FY26 +$67.4M YoY → Q3 FY26 +$130.0M YoY — doubling of incremental growth
shifts.note: 9M FY26 CS data center up $221.7M YoY; hyperscale/colocation in NA and Europe cited as primary markets

shifts.trend: Climate Solutions op margin: Q2 13.7% (-440bps YoY) → Q3 15.3% (-200bps YoY) — improving as capacity ramp matures
shifts.note: Margin recovery from capacity ramp inefficiencies; SG&A leverage improving; full normalization expected as US DC capacity utilization builds

shifts.trend: Performance Technologies: Q2 -4% → Q3 not detailed (RMT announced Jan 2026); MOD restructuring into pure-play data center cooling company
shifts.note: RMT value ~$1.0B; MOD receives $210M cash at close; shareholders get ~40% of combined Gentherm — value extraction from PT while focusing on DC

shifts.trend: Q2: announced incremental $100M DC capex over next 12 months; Q3: incremental DC manufacturing lease signed; 9M capex +$19.1M YoY
shifts.note: Pure-play DC cooling transformation requires facility expansion; US manufacturing scale-up driving capex step-up

MOD.verdict: Thesis intact. MOD's data center revenue doubling sequentially from $67M to $130M incremental YoY growth demonstrates the secular demand. The RMT spin is transformative — divesting PT for $1B equivalent value while crystallizing a pure-play DC cooling multiple. Climate Solutions margin recovery from 13.7% to 15.3% indicates the US capacity ramp is maturing. Fiscal Q4 2026 (Mar-Jun) will be the first full-quarter of the new structure.

MRVL.headline: FY26 Data Center $6.1B (+46%), gross margin +9.7pts to 51%; Celestial AI + XConn acquisitions post year-end add photonic fabric and CXL switching.

shifts.trend: Data center end-market: Q3 FY26 $1.52B (73%, +38% YoY) → FY26 full-year $6.1B (74%, +46% YoY) — full-year acceleration vs quarterly run-rate
shifts.note: Custom AI ASIC + electro-optics the primary growth engine; Communications & other $2.1B (+31%) recovering from inventory correction

shifts.trend: Gross margin: Q3 51.6% (prior year Q3 was 23% from $356.8M impairments) → FY26 51.0% — clean underlying margin now >50% at scale
shifts.note: Automotive sale to Infineon ($2.5B, $1.8B gain Q3) removed a lower-margin business; remaining portfolio is higher-margin AI/DC

shifts.trend: Post-FY26 acquisitions: Celestial AI (~$1.3B + 24.5M shares, Feb 2026) and XConn (Feb 2026) for photonic fabric and CXL/PCIe switching
shifts.note: 5nm in production, 3nm in design, 2nm/1.4nm on roadmap — technology cadence positioning for next-gen XPU cycles

shifts.trend: Q3: Distributor A = 38% of revenue (hyperscaler channel); FY26: Distributor A = 37% — concentration stable and tied to hyperscaler XPU cycles
shifts.note: Direct Customer A at 13-14% provides some direct-to-hyperscaler revenue ballast; overall concentration remains elevated

MRVL.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening. MRVL's custom AI ASIC franchise is the primary alternative XPU platform alongside AMD for hyperscalers seeking NVDA alternatives. The Celestial AI acquisition (photonic fabric) is a forward bet on the CPO/silicon photonics interconnect transition. 51% gross margins at scale validate the premium positioning. The Distributor A concentration (37%) remains the key operational risk.

MSFT.headline: Azure growth re-accelerating from +39% to +40%; commercial RPO $625-627B stable; H1 capex $49.3B vs $30.7B prior year; OpenAI Recapitalization gain booked.

shifts.trend: Azure: Q2 FY26 +39% → Q3 FY26 +40% — sequential re-acceleration; Microsoft Cloud: Q2 $51.5B (+26%) → Q3 $54.5B (+29%)
shifts.note: 9M Azure growth pacing above FY25; OpenAI extended partnership (Oct 2025) locked in exclusive inference relationship

shifts.trend: Q2 FY26 capex $29.9B (+89% YoY); Q3 FY26 $30.9B; 9M $80.1B vs $40.9B prior year (+96%) — nearly doubling YoY
shifts.note: Commercial RPO stable: $625B (Q2) → $627B (Q3) after massive new contract signings; $631B at Dec 31 2025 total RPO

shifts.trend: Microsoft Cloud gross margin: Q2 67% (down from 72% FY25) on AI infra investments; Q3 trend continuing; operating margin +20% YoY in dollar terms
shifts.note: Cloud GM compression from AI infra investment is intentional and guided; absolute operating income growing faster than revenue

shifts.trend: $5.9B 9M non-operating gain from OpenAI Recapitalization dilution gain (OpenAI converted to public benefit corp); M365 Consumer cloud +33%
shifts.note: MSFT uniquely benefits from OpenAI equity appreciation + Azure revenue from OpenAI — double exposure to AI monetization

MSFT.verdict: Thesis intact and compounding. Azure +40% re-acceleration with $627B RPO and capex doubling YoY is the strongest demand signal in the sector. MSFT has the dual benefit of being both the AI infrastructure provider (Azure) and the AI application monetizer (Copilot/M365/GitHub). The OpenAI recapitalization gain ($5.9B) is a one-time but validation of equity position. Cloud GM compression is the only watch item.

MXL.headline: Recovery to +43% YoY in Q1 2026 from +30% FY25 trough; Infrastructure (optical DSP) +$36.3M leading; 1.6T transition the next-gen optical DSP catalyst.

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 $467.6M (+30% YoY, recovery from trough) → Q1 2026 $137.2M (+43% YoY); Infrastructure segment leading both periods
shifts.note: FY25 recovery driven by broadband and infrastructure; Q1 2026 infrastructure +$36.3M includes optical DSP traction

shifts.trend: Top customer: FY25 >20% → Q1 2026 13%; top 10: FY25 65% → Q1 2026 56% — customer diversification improving meaningfully
shifts.note: Broadening customer base is bullish for revenue quality; suggests optical DSP products winning new hyperscaler accounts

shifts.trend: Gross margin: FY25 57% (+3pts) on mix and lower intangible amortization; Q1 2026 trend similar given volume-led growth
shifts.note: R&D -7% in FY25 from workforce reductions; mgmt notes R&D re-expansion needed for next-gen products — future cost pressure expected

shifts.trend: Infrastructure +$34.3M (FY25) vs +$36.3M (Q1 2026, quarterly) — quarterly run-rate now exceeding the full-year increment; 400G→800G→1.6T roadmap explicit
shifts.note: MoCA/Wi-Fi Connectivity and Broadband recovering from inventory cycle; Industrial/multi-market -$37M FY25 still weak

MXL.verdict: Recovery thesis with upside from 1.6T optical DSP cycle. MXL is in early innings of the 800G→1.6T optical interconnect transition that should drive multi-year infrastructure revenue growth. Customer diversification improving (top customer down from >20% to 13%) is a positive structural shift. Main risk: limited scale vs MACOM/Coherent in InP optical chips; MXL's optical DSP must prove hyperscaler adoption at 1.6T speeds.

NOW.headline: RPO +27% to $28.2B (FY25) → +25% to $27.7B (Q1 2026); subscription revenue +21% consistently; Now Assist AI agents deeply embedded across ITSM/CSM product suite.

shifts.note: RPO slight decline from $28.2B to $27.7B sequentially — seasonal pattern; cRPO +23% YoY showing consistent forward demand

shifts.trend: US federal self-hosted deployments driving $83M FY25 incremental ($492M total upfront); Q1 2026 self-hosted $138M (slightly below Q1 FY25 $157M)
shifts.note: AI agent (Now Assist) consumption pricing is an unknown — new revenue model not yet material in reported figures

shifts.trend: Subscription gross margin stable; self-hosted mix slightly compressing in Q1 2026; FY25 OBBBA enacted — immediate domestic R&D expensing benefit
shifts.note: Operating leverage from subscription model + lower SBC dilution driving EPS growth faster than revenue

NOW.verdict: Thesis intact. ServiceNow is the enterprise AI workflow automation platform that benefits from AI agent adoption without requiring capex. Now Assist embedded across 9+ product lines. The +22% subscription revenue growth is durable and the RPO ($27.7B) provides multi-year visibility. Main risk: consumption-based AI agent pricing remains 'relatively new and uncertain' per mgmt — upside but also uncertainty on monetization.

NVT.headline: Systems Protection organic +17% (FY25) to +50% (Q1 FY26); backlog $2.3B; EPG acquisition contributing; capex doubling to $425-525M for capacity expansion.

shifts.trend: Systems Protection organic growth: FY25 ~17% (+16pt from infrastructure) → Q1 FY26 +50.1% organic; total Q1 revenue $1,242M (+53.5%)
shifts.note: Infrastructure vertical: FY25 ~16pt contribution → Q1 FY26 ~46pt contribution to organic growth — massive acceleration in DC infra demand

shifts.trend: Systems Protection segment margin: FY25 20.7% (-1.4pts from capacity investment) → Q1 FY26 22.7% (+2.0pts) — recovering as capacity utilizes
shifts.note: Gross margin pressure: FY25 37.7% (-2.5pts) → Q1 FY26 35.9% (-2.9pts) — tariffs and raw material persisting; operating leverage improving

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $226.4M → FY26 guidance $425-525M; Avail EPG acquisition ($1.0B, May 2025); Thermal Management divested to Brookfield ($1.6B, Jan 2025)
shifts.note: Portfolio: sold lower-margin TM, bought higher-growth EPG, doubled capex — pure-play data center enclosures/liquid cooling pivot

shifts.trend: No single customer >10% disclosed; backlog $2.3B driven by EPG + DC vertical demand; substantially all to ship in 2026
shifts.note: Backlog growth suggests NVT's data center exposure is contracted, not spot — provides revenue visibility

NVT.verdict: Thesis intact. NVT's 50% organic growth in Systems Protection in Q1 FY26 is extraordinary and validates the DC enclosures + liquid cooling demand thesis. The EPG acquisition + $425-525M capex commitment show management conviction. Gross margin compression from tariffs is a watch item but operating leverage is improving. The Thermal Management divestiture ($1.6B) was smart capital allocation.

OKLO.headline: Order book 18,100 MWe → Meta 1.2 GW Pike County prepayment (Jan 2026); Aurora safety design approved by DOE; 2028 first powerhouse deployment target maintained.

shifts.trend: Order book: ~18,100 MWe (Q3 FY25) growing; Jan 2026 added Meta prepayment for 1.2 GW Pike County; Switch 12 GW master + Equinix/Diamondback LOIs in place
shifts.note: Pre-revenue company; order book growth is the only financial leading indicator; $1.84B implied ARR at commercial scale if all orders convert

shifts.trend: 9M FY25 cash used in investing $605.9M; FY25 10-K: targets FY26 cash burn $80-100M, capex $350-450M for Pike County; $300M ATM raised Dec 2025
shifts.note: FY26 capex $350-450M is a major step-up — Pike County land acquired, Kiewit as lead constructor for fuel recycling facility

shifts.trend: Q3: NRC Phase I pre-application no significant gaps; Q4 FY25: Aurora Nuclear Safety Design Agreement approved by DOE; 3 of 11 DOE Reactor Pilot Program projects awarded
shifts.note: Regulatory momentum is the critical path for OKLO — faster NRC approval = earlier commercial deployment = faster order book conversion

shifts.trend: Cash $1.18B (Q3 FY25); FY25 accumulated deficit $199.3M; Aug 2025 issued $300M+ ATM; burn rate $48.7M 9M ops + capex investment
shifts.note: Meta prepayment agreement (amount undisclosed) may provide construction capital ahead of DOE loan guarantee — financial risk materially reduced

OKLO.verdict: Speculative thesis with improving probability of commercialization. The Meta prepayment for 1.2 GW is the most concrete demand signal yet — a hyperscaler is paying money ahead of production for nuclear power. DOE design approval removes a major binary risk. The 2028 first powerhouse target is 2.5 years away — execution risk is high but funding risk is materially lower now. Value zero or multi-billion — binary.

ORCL.headline: OCI revenue +68% to +84% in two quarters; RPO exploding from $97B to $552B YoY — largest hyperscaler demand signal in software; capex running 3x prior year.

shifts.trend: Cloud infrastructure (OCI): Q2 FY26 $4.08B (+68% YoY) → Q3 FY26 $4.89B (+84% YoY) — sequential acceleration; 9M capex $39.2B (3.2x prior year)
shifts.note: RPO: $97.3B (Nov 24 YoY) → $523.3B (Nov 25) → $552.6B (Feb 26) — 5.7x in 12 months; driven by massive multi-year AI cloud contracts

shifts.trend: H1 FY26 capex $20.5B vs $6.3B H1 FY25 (+225%); Q3 9M total $39.2B; mgmt: 'upward trend to continue next few fiscal years'
shifts.note: Issued $42.7B senior notes + $5B mandatory converts to fund the build; capital-intensive phase is structural, not one-time

shifts.trend: Debt issuance: $17.9B Sept 2025 + $42.7B FY26 total; Ampere stake sold $4.3B; cloud and software segment expenses +49% on infrastructure
shifts.note: Operating margin % declining as infrastructure spend ramps ahead of revenue recognition — classic cloud build-out P&L pattern

shifts.trend: RPO jumped $29.3B in one quarter (Q2 to Q3) as new AI contracts signed; Q2 RPO leapt $426B in one year — most extraordinary RPO expansion across the coverage
shifts.note: Oracle's multi-cloud strategy (AWS/Azure/ORCL all hosting OCI nodes) is enabling hyperscaler-within-a-hyperscaler architecture

ORCL.verdict: Thesis strongly intact. ORCL's RPO explosion from $97B to $552B in 12 months is the most dramatic demand signal in the AI cloud infrastructure sector. OCI +84% growth is driven by hyperscalers provisioning GPU clusters through Oracle's multi-cloud nodes. The leverage build ($42.7B notes) is the key risk — ORCL is funding a $39B+ annual capex program on a business generating ~$20B operating income. Execution on revenue recognition will determine whether the capex is dilutive or accretive.

PLTR.headline: Revenue +53% FY25 to +85% Q1 2026; operating margin 20% → 46% in 15 months; commercial revenue now +95% — the growth driver shifted from government to commercial AI.

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 $4.5B (+53%) → Q1 2026 $1.63B run-rate (+85% YoY) — 32pt acceleration in one quarter; commercial +95%, government +76%
shifts.note: US revenue 79% of Q1 vs 74% FY25 — US commercial AI bootcamp model converting enterprise accounts at scale

shifts.trend: Operating income: FY25 $1.41B (31% margin) → Q1 2026 $754M (+328% YoY); operating margin 20% → 46% — extraordinary inflection
shifts.note: Gross margin Q1 87% (vs 80% Q1 25) + operating leverage on high-fixed-cost software model = extreme operating leverage

shifts.trend: Commercial: FY25 $2.07B (+60%) → Q1 2026 $774M (+95%); Government contribution margin 73% (vs 62% FY25); Commercial 73% (vs 61%)
shifts.note: Both segments hitting 73% contribution margin simultaneously — PLTR's AI platform (AIP/Foundry/Gotham) scaling into both channels

shifts.trend: No customer >10% of Q1 2026 or Q1 2025 revenue — improved vs prior years when US government dominated; international growing but small
shifts.note: AIP bootcamp model is creating a long tail of enterprise customers across both government and commercial — diversification structural

PLTR.verdict: Thesis strongly intact. PLTR's operating margin at 46% on +85% revenue growth is the most exceptional combination in enterprise software. The commercial acceleration to +95% confirms AI adoption is crossing the chasm from pilot to production in enterprise. The main structural concern is whether PLTR's 73% contribution margins are sustainable as competition from hyperscaler AI services (Azure OpenAI, Google Vertex) intensifies.

PWR.headline: Backlog $43.98B (+27.3%) → $48.47B (+10.2% QoQ); RPO $23.76B → $26.24B; Electric segment +21% on DC-driven utility demand; DSI acquisition adds inside-DC exposure.

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 $28.5B (+20.3%) → Q1 FY26 $7.87B (+26.3% YoY); backlog: $43.98B (YE25) → $48.47B (Q1 end) +10.2% in one quarter
shifts.note: Q1 backlog growth $4.5B in one quarter — the fastest backlog build in PWR's history; data center driving both Electric and Underground segments

shifts.trend: RPO: $23.76B (YE25, +41.8% YoY) → $26.24B (Q1 end, +10.4% QoQ) — contracted future revenue growing faster than current revenue
shifts.note: RPO/backlog gap implies significant post-order work not yet under specific contract — a leading indicator of future revenue acceleration

shifts.trend: Q1 FY26 gross margin 14.1% (vs 13.4%) +70bps; operating margin 4.3% (vs 3.8%) +50bps — margins expanding on revenue scale
shifts.note: Operating income +41.7% outpacing +26.3% revenue growth — significant operating leverage in construction/engineering services at scale

shifts.trend: FY25: $3.30B in acquisitions (CEI, DSI); DSI ($1.7B, July 2025) adds mechanical/plumbing/process for hyperscaler/semi/healthcare facilities — inside-DC exposure
shifts.note: Turnkey DC infrastructure capability: low-voltage inside DC (CEI) + HV substation/transformer + fiber + civil — now fully integrated

PWR.verdict: Thesis intact and strengthening. PWR's $48.47B backlog and $26.24B RPO — with both growing at double-digit quarterly rates — provide extraordinary multi-year revenue visibility. Q1 +41.7% operating income growth vs +26.3% revenue is the thesis in numbers. DSI adds the inside-DC capability that completes a turnkey hyperscaler infrastructure offering. Main risk: labor availability for the construction ramp.

SNOW.headline: NRR 125% stable; $1M+ customers +27% to 733; product revenue +29% consistently; RPO $9.8B (+24%); net loss narrowing as SBC ratio falls.

shifts.trend: Product revenue: Q3 FY26 $1.16B (+29%) → FY26 full year ~$4.5B implied (+31%); total customers 13,328 (+21%); $1M+ customers 733 (+27%)
shifts.note: Forbes Global 2000 customers: 790 at ~43% of revenue — enterprise penetration deepening at the highest-value tier

shifts.trend: SBC/revenue: FY26 ~34% vs FY25 41% — declining; net loss $1.3B FY26; operating cash flow $1.2B positive — cash-generative despite GAAP loss
shifts.note: SBC ratio declining is key to the free-cash-flow bull case; at <30% SBC/revenue, SNOW becomes meaningfully FCF-positive on a normalized basis

shifts.trend: NRR: Q3 FY26 125% → FY26 year-end 125% — flat; RPO: $7.9B (Oct 31) → $9.8B (Jan 31) +24% sequential build
shifts.note: Consumption-based model makes NRR a lagging indicator of GPU/AI workload growth on Snowflake; RPO is the better leading indicator

shifts.trend: OBBBA enacted July 2025 — immediate domestic R&D expensing benefit; ASC 985-20 self-hosted accounting for public sector expanded in Q1 FY26
shifts.note: Public sector self-hosted product adds a non-consumption revenue model; convertible notes trigger met Q3 — potential equity dilution signal

SNOW.verdict: Thesis intact. SNOW's consumption-based AI data workload platform is the infrastructure layer for AI training and inference data pipelines. The $1M+ customer count +27% and NRR 125% are durable enterprise retention metrics. The SBC ratio declining to 34% is the path to reported profitability. Main risk: hyperscaler-native data warehousing (BigQuery, Redshift) competing directly with Snowflake on cost at large scales.

SNPS.headline: Ansys ($34.9B) closed Jul 2025 — revenue +66% YoY; $13.5B debt being paid down aggressively; Design IP weak on China BIS + foundry softness; Processor IP divested to GFS.

shifts.trend: Revenue: FY25 $7.05B (+15%, with Ansys $756.6M in 4.5 months) → Q1 FY26 $2.41B (+66% YoY, Ansys $885.6M full quarter)
shifts.note: Q1 FY26 Design Automation +96% (Ansys-driven); Design IP -6% on China BIS + foundry customer softness — bifurcated segment performance

shifts.trend: FY25: China -22% ex-Ansys on May 2025 BIS 'is-informed' letter; Q1 FY26: BIS rescinded Jul 2 2025 — China recovery in progress; design IP foundry softness lingering
shifts.note: CDNS had same China BIS whiplash and saw +36% YoY recovery; SNPS should see similar if foundry design pipeline normalizes

shifts.trend: Total debt: $13.5B at FY25 end → Q1 FY26: $3.5B term loan paid off; aggressive paydown before buyback resumption
shifts.note: $300-350M FY26 restructuring plan ('2026 Plan') for post-Ansys workforce reduction; $118.3M Q1 restructuring charge taken

shifts.trend: Operating income: FY25 $914.9M (-32% from $1.36B prior, Ansys amortization + integration costs) → Q1 FY26 $203M (8.4% margin) compressed by $394.1M Ansys amortization
shifts.note: Ansys intangible amortization will run at $394M+ per quarter for years — reported margins structurally compressed vs organic SNPS

SNPS.verdict: Thesis intact on the core EDA thesis. The Ansys acquisition adds multi-physics simulation (chips-to-system-to-environment) which is uniquely valuable for AI chip thermal management and power analysis at advanced nodes. However, the $13.5B debt + $394M/quarter amortization means reported earnings will look poor for 3-5 years. Investors need to look at cash EBITDA, not GAAP EPS. Design IP weakness is a temporary headwind from China BIS and foundry cycle.

VRT.headline: Americas +42% (FY25) to +53% (Q1 FY26); combined backlog doubled to $15B; gross margin expanded 400bps to 37.7%; capex nearly doubling for capacity.

shifts.note: Backlog doubled from $7.2B (YE24) to $15.0B (YE25) to still growing in Q1 FY26 — hyperscaler liquid cooling orders booked ahead of capacity

shifts.trend: Gross margin: FY25 36.3% (-30bps on tariffs) → Q1 FY26 37.7% (+400bps YoY) — strong sequential recovery; Americas op margin 26.8% (FY25) → 27.0% (Q1 FY26)
shifts.note: 400bps gross margin expansion in Q1 on volume leverage and favorable mix; tariff headwinds from FY25 are being absorbed by pricing and scale

shifts.trend: FY25 capex $226.4M → FY26 guidance $425-525M (nearly doubling); Q1 FY26 $114M deployed; $2.15B cash + $2.48B revolver available for the build
shifts.note: Capacity investment in Americas is the primary spend; mgmt cites 'very robust' AI/HPC demand for HVDC and hybrid air-liquid cooling architectures

shifts.trend: Americas 68% of Q1 FY26 revenue (vs 62% FY25); EMEA -20.3% as European AI buildout lags Americas; Asia Pacific +14.9%
shifts.note: US hyperscaler concentration is a strength in this cycle but creates geographic vulnerability if US data center capex pulls back

VRT.verdict: Thesis strongly intact. VRT is the purest-play liquid cooling company for AI data centers. The 400bps gross margin expansion to 37.7%, +53% Americas growth, $15B backlog (doubled), and nearly doubling capex tell a consistent story of accelerating demand with improving pricing power. The HVDC (high-voltage DC) architecture adoption is VRT's next growth vector. Main risk: if cooling technology shifts to a competitor's architecture (immersion vs liquid-to-chip), VRT could lose share.
